summary: Found exposure of multiple high-risk PII fields, including insurance_id, phone_number, and precise geolocation (latitude/longitude) for every crash record. Persistent crash_record_id enables potential IDOR attacks.
business_impact: High risk of privacy violations (e.g., GDPR/CCPA equivalents, driver data breach). Could lead to regulatory fines, loss of public trust, and legal liability for the municipal entity.

findings.title: Geospatial PII Leak
findings.description: Precise latitude and longitude are available for every incident without authentication. This allows for exact identification of crash sites, including residential driveways.
findings.recommendation: Implement Differential Privacy: Apply noise to the latitude/longitude fields for public-facing API queries.

findings.title: IDOR Vulnerability
findings.description: The crash_record_id is a persistent hash. If used as a primary key in other internal workflows (like 'Driver Portal'), it could be used for unauthorized record enumeration.
findings.recommendation: Use unpredictable, time-limited, or access-scoped identifiers for public APIs.

recommendations: Implement proper authentication (OAuth/JWT) and authorization checks on all report-related endpoints.
Redact or pseudonymize PII in public/preview workflows.
Use server-side rendering with access controls; avoid client-side exposure of raw data.
Add rate limiting, logging, and monitoring for anomalous access.
Conduct regular penetration testing and privacy impact assessments (PIA).
Consider token-based access tied to verified involved-party status.
Implement Differential Privacy for geospatial fields.
